title: Default Systems

content.statement: What happens by default dominates your life.
content.question: What's one default you could engineer that would save 10 decisions a day?
sop: Define what your ideal day or ideal outcome actually looks like in specifics.
Rate your current Default 1-10, how well the automatic behavior matches that ideal (see calculation, page 5)
Build one piece of automation or a standing rule that removes a repeated decision entirely.
Remove the manual decision point completely, don't just add willpower on top of it.
Review defaults quarterly; anything you're still deciding manually is a candidate to automate next.
questions: What's one default you could engineer that would save 10 decisions a day?
If you did absolutely nothing different this year, what would your default trajectory actually produce?
Which decision do you re-make every single day that you could pre-decide once and never touch again?

definitions.def: The behavior that happens when no decision is made.

content.equation_example: Rate your Default 1-10 (how well the automatic behavior serves you). Default 8 times Time 12 (months it's been running) equals 96 outcome-points accumulated. Higher means a strong default is compounding a good result without requiring willpower. Lower means a weak default is quietly compounding damage the same way.
content.closing_statement: What you default to is what you actually value.

caption:
Principle Nº 133: Default Systems

What happens by default dominates your life.

Ask yourself: What's one default you could engineer that would save 10 decisions a day?
